speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three-- October 10th, 1996-- interview with Mrs. Pearl Barach. So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Skarzysko, we stayed there. I would say, they took us out in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '40-- '40-- '43, I think. No-- yes, in 43, right. And they took us to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not far from the-- from the German border. There were also ammunition factories there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was also a camp? You were in the camp over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the same camp-- the same camp for ammunition. It was a camp, and then they took us to work, you know, every day and then brought us back. We walked, you know. We had to walk, and I stayed there. You know, they thought they'll take us out to Germany, right, because the Russians came closer. But they could not cross the Vistula River near Wisla. This was a suburb of Wisla. So they couldn't-- it took them six months to cross. So we stayed there and worked the six months. Once they crossed Wisla, they tried to ship us out to Germany. They shipped out-- not everybody at one time because there were a lot of people. And they also had three factories there-- three camps-- [GERMAN], [? Wartenberg, ?] and [INAUDIBLE]. And I was in the [GERMAN] near the Warta River. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I saw once there was a Bartenschlager, his name was. I don't know that a man was standing there, and he did something. He said-- probably said he did something wrong if they didn't-- so he put them near the water with a guard, and he had to look into the water for so long till it fell in and he drowned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I saw myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were-- who was guarding the workers over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- German, Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them show any act of kindness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they are beatings here too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. If you did something-- it was an ammunition factory. If they didn't come out right, it was your fault.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a night did you-- were you able to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had enough time, but who could sleep? We could not sleep. We slept on one-- you know, the-- on one bed-- three people. Who could sleep?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there about six months-- July, right-- about six months. And then they crossed the river, and they start coming closer. They-- they tried to-- to send people to Germany, or to other camps, I don't know. And not every day-- every day, but not everybody at the same time. They couldn't. There were too many people. And it came to my turn. So they took us, and they put us in a big barn. And they locked us up, and they said we are waiting for the trains. And we were waiting and waiting and waiting, and they opened the doors. They say-- and they said, you are going back to the barrack-- the camp-- and that that's a good sign. You'll be happy. We come to the camp. We don't see any Germans. They all ran away. The Russians came in, but they didn't do nothing for us because they were fighting. They were chasing them-- the Germans. And there were a lot of people-- mature people-- lawyers, doctors-- at camp. This was in evening already. He said, let's stay overnight. We have no place where to go. We don't-- and we'll see what to do in the morning. And they had speeches-- we are liberated, and this, you know. In the morning, we went out just to see what's going on outside. So the Polish people-- they didn't know what happened. The soldiers were laying dead-- the Russians and Germans on the ground. They were fighting. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people ask us, did they-- did they let you out, Jews? So we said, no, the Germans ran away. They left us. We were there just another few days. And I said my brother jumped. My father may be there alive. And I-- we tried to go back. I tried, and my-- and other friends too-- whoever-- you know, we survived from our town-- friends. And we tried to get back, but we stayed here a few days. I don't know-- I don't remember what we ate. We had no food either.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went on the trains, but they transport wood. There were no walls, no nothing, no roof-- only four poles in each-- in each corner, a pole-- four poles. And we were like-- we were squeezed like a box of [INAUDIBLE]. We could not sit down. We could not stand up. And we had-- everything we have to do it-- and we had no clothes-- not too much. I had a little raincoat-- not lined-- and it was in January. And in Poland, it's cold. And we came to a-- I don't know how long it took, maybe a day or two-- and we came to a station-- Lukow-- it was a nice Jewish town. But this wasn't in town, the station. And we already got in. They let us in the passenger trains. And we went to our-- we went to-- it was a few hours, maybe two hours, to get home by train-- to-- the station was called [GERMAN]-- our town. And we got off. I saw people, and they recognized me-- Gentiles. They-- everybody wanted to take us home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got on the, you know, on the buggy, and they took us home. But there-- I had no home. But then when I came to my street, I saw my cousin-- the one who jumped from the train. He survived. And I said, Shimon, Shimon! I start yelling, Shimon! And he was looking at me, where is she coming from? He didn't know. They were liberated in 1944. And this was-- you were liberated when?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45 in January-- the 17th or the 18th. I think it was the 17th. And I was yelling. He recognized me, and he came over. And he says, you know, [GERMAN] is alive-- your cousin [GERMAN] and his wife. I said, oh, that's good. I said, where is my father and brother? He didn't answer me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I asked him again. He said, if you're not going to say nothing-- don't say nothing. Don't yell. Don't scream. And he told me the story of what happened. The Polack killed them. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Polish man that killed your father and brother? Was he still in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I didn't stay too long in the town because we were afraid to stay there after-- because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed four weeks, but I didn't-- I didn't see him because we didn't go there so far, you know. But he told me who he was. He knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me what happened there in your hometown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was there about four weeks. And I looked a little better. I gained a little weight. I was-- I stayed with my cousin and his wife. She was four months pregnant. And there were another few Jews there-- a few in other houses, not there, not by us. I stayed with them for four weeks. And one night, it was Purim night. My cousin put out fish to cook because we had to go make a [INAUDIBLE]. And I was laying down on the daybed in the kitchen, and I put up water to warm. I was going to wash my hair, and I heard a knock on the door. I heard a knock, and I opened the door. And he asked me if he could have some water. I got up. I gave him a glass of water. And he was holding the water in his hand, and he went towards the door to the bedroom because my cousin always had the Russian soldiers there. They used to come into him, so he was checking. And as soon as he saw there was no one there, he put away the glass. And he said, raise your hands-- and he pulled his gun-- and don't say nothing. So we did. He told us to go in to the bedroom. To the man, he said, lay down, face on the floor-- face down-- the women too. And to me, you know, he was sitting on a chair like this with a gun holding. I saw one man. And then other men came-- other. They were dressed in Polish uniforms. This was the Armia Krajowa-- National Army-- an underground army. And they didn't like Jews. And there were another few men. And I guess some were guarding outside. I don't know. I didn't see. But I know because they were watching them. And they start looking in the closets and taking out things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he asked me-- he told me to sit down near the table. I was-- you know, I'm short. I was-- I sat down, and he asked me my name. I told him. And he asked me where I was. I said I was in the concentration camps. He said, yes, how long? I said, almost two years. He said, yes, you suffered in concentration camp, and now you're-- you're going to jail. I said to him, I am not afraid to go to jail. I did nothing wrong. And I prayed to God. They should send me to jail because I saw something else-- I saw the guns and everything. So then after this, they were-- I could not tell time because every minute is like a year. And they told me also to lay down to face. And they went in to load up the guns-- I think machine guns were they. And they start shooting. Yeah, and my cousin said to me-- to me and the other girls-- scream, yell, scream! You know, we should start-- and nothing-- like, God wanted me to live-- nothing came out of me. I could not scream. I wanted, really, to scream, but I couldn't. Nothing came out. And they-- and the people were screaming. And they-- they killed them all-- six people. And I was the only survivor. What were the names of the people in that apartment that were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin's name was [GERMAN]. His wife was [GERMAN]. She was four months pregnant. My other cousins was Shimon Friedman, Pearl [GERMAN, [GERMAN], and a little boy-- he was seven years old-- he survived with his mother on a Polish passport. They were in Wisla. But at that night, he was seven years old. He was so gorgeous. And at that-- at this night, she didn't have-- they didn't have much room where they stayed. She stayed with other people. So she sent them to us, and he asked me if he can sleep over here. I said, of course. So that's why he was there. And they killed all the six. His name was [GERMAN]. The second I remember. I don't remember the first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Where did you go after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were afraid already. I don't know who buried them. But you know, I was all shook-- shaken up. And we went to a different town-- to Radzyn. And we stayed there-- the people who survived, they didn't want to stay in this town no more. We were afraid. And we lived there with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Polish people treat the Jews after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- while we were staying there, he brought us-- one friend brought us food-- butter, bread-- but they had that anyway-- my cousin had, I think. And then in Radzyn, too, they used to bring me. And he was very nice to-- the man who-- from whom we rented the mill. They were nice to me too. They wanted me to stay with them. When I came home from concentration camp, they wanted me to stay with them. But I said, I'm going to stay with Gentiles if I have a cousin a Jew? So I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel a lot through Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to Katowice. I had a friend living in Miedzyrzec where we had the-- the ghetto was no more ghetto. And we went to visit not too much. I was-- once they killed them, I was all alone again. Only when I went to Katowice, I stayed there. And in 1946, we went to-- to Chechoslovakia, and we came to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany? [BACKGROUND NOISE] In a DP camp in Bad Reichenhall in Bavaria and in the Alps. It was close to the Austrian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Jews treated in DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the DP camps, they were treated-- they got food and everything and clothes. They were treated good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the mood of the Jews? Where did they want to go from the DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some went to Israel, and some wanted to go to America. We really wanted to go to Israel. But we had-- my husband had relatives, and they kept on writing to us that they want us. And in the meantime, they sent us to Savannah, Georgia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the day you met your husband. What-- what was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam-- [HEBREW] in Hebrew. How I met him-- he come-- he came to this DP camp. He used to live in [GERMAN] by Nuremberg. And he used to come to business with cigarettes, you know, they had to live. So he used to come by there. And I met him. And my friend was also married to his [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the wedding. Who married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who married-- a rabbi in a hotel in Bad Reichenhall town. Yes, that was not a concentration camp. It was a DP camp. This was Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Jewish life resume after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had shuls. They had everything. [BACKGROUNG NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about when you came to the United States. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- they sent me to Savannah, Georgia. And I was pregnant when I came. First, we came to New Orleans, and they were waiting for us. And they made us a big party. We came on the boat, I remember, General Hershey. It was an army boat. And the women took us around, showed us New Orleans. And then they took us to this station, and we came to Savannah. Other people waited for us because we didn't speak English, and we didn't know the country. And-- and they put us in a hotel for a few days until they found an apartment for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became part of the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we became-- there were three shuls there. Every Friday, we went for our Oneg Shabbat. There was a Reformed, a Conservative, and an Orthodox shul. And they treated us very nice, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Savannah, Georgia, about 2 and 1/2 years. And my-- and my husband wanted to go because he had relatives there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you moved--

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved, right. I had only one baby. He was two years and one month old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, yes, to Brooklyn. We got a furnished apartment, and he got a job in a luncheonette. He worked in Savannah, Georgia too. They gave him a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was life good for you and your husband in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was good until he started making a little more money.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were raising your children, did you tell them the stories-- your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I could not. When they were small, I could not. I don't think I ever told them the whole story anyway. I cannot talk about it. But they know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your sons. What did you try to teach them? What was important for you to teach them while you were-- while they were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, they should be honest, good people. And they went to school. They finished-- and I sent them to [INAUDIBLE]. Not when they were very small. And I tried to teach them the best that I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I always think. I was very young when I lost both parents-- the whole family. I lost everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What memories or visions come to your mind about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a good life when I was home before the war. I had everything I needed and everything I wanted. We were very comfortable and happy. I played with my brothers. Sometimes we were fighting, you know, like children. But they were very protective of me because my little sister-- I was one and four brothers. And then my little sister was born 1938. But until then, they were very protective of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you what do you dream about in your nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes I'm in concentration camp. They're chasing me. I'm running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever feel joy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm always sad. In the beginning, well, my husband was alive. It was different. Now, it's-- it's different too. When the children were small, I was busy, you know, with them. I tried to do the best and everything for them. They should be-- taking good care, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you have for future generations about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: They should never forget what happened, and they should not let it happen. It is true. I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much for sharing your story with me today. Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This picture is my brother Eliezer's from the before the war. He sent to Israel in 1938. This is a picture from before the war from my class from Bais Yaakov. I am on the top row-- the fourth. This is a picture taken in Bad Reichenhall in the DP camp, and I am on the bottom row-- the second from left. This-- this is a picture from my wedding-- my husband, Sam Barach. It was taken in Bad Reichenhall in 1949, April 10th. This is a picture-- me and my husband. We took a cruise to Bermuda in 1991 in May. You can see his number on his left arm from concentration camp. This is the whole picture for my family taken at a wedding-- Ali's wedding. From top row from the left is Ali, my daughter-in-law, David, my son, Alan, and then my daughter-in-law, Kathy, and my middle son Robert. And standing from left on the bottom is my grandson Michael. My husband Sam is sitting, and I'm next to him. This is my granddaughter Samantha and my grandson Jake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Barach, could you please introduce your son to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Alan Mark Barach.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special message for your mother or something you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I'd like to say that I love her very much. And I thought she was probably going to kill me when I encouraged her to do this tape, but I think one of the values that we'll get out of this down the line is that to see-- I know it wasn't easy for her, but for my children and their children and schools or wherever these tapes are viewed, they can look down a hundred years from now and see that somebody-- that it wasn't in vain. She came over. She survived. And I think the hardest part first of her surviving and my father as well was, daily-- was-- what they did was to push the family, to rebuild, really, to rebuild. And going forward, you know, I'm thankful for it because I'm here and my grandchildren and great grandchildren-- generations that I won't see-- can know that there was one limb left on the tree, and hopefully that will sprout again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three minutes. Prince.
